system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 5: Module 4 - Arranging For Solo Guitar (Techniques)
Pages 137-199 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

Module 4 - Arranging
  for Solo Guitar


Introduction to Arranging for Solo Guitar

You’re at an exciting point in this course – solo jazz guitar arranging is where a lot of creative
possibilities can open up in your guitar playing.

There’s nothing quite like the feeling of being able to play a rich, intricate arrangement as a complete
performance by yourself, on a single guitar.

The good news:

You’ve done most of the hard work already. All the techniques we learned for basic “trio” chord
melody arranging we covered earlier in the course can still be applied to solo jazz guitar versions of
tunes.

We only need to add a few extra elements to make your arrangements sound good on guitar
unaccompanied.




The “Illusion” of Solo Jazz Guitar

The aim of solo jazz guitar performance is to give the audience an experience of a whole band on a
single guitar – bass, rhythm section, and top melody line.

Here’s the thing:

It is not necessary to have all these elements at all times in an arrangement. (That usually makes the
arrangement way too difficult).

A good solo guitar arranger simply suggests these elements throughout the performance. Play a
measure of walking bass, followed by a chord line, followed by unaccompanied single lines –
alternating through these different devices gives the ‘illusion’ to the audience of a complete band.

The listener’s mind will fill in the gaps.


Pick vs Fingers for Solo Guitar

As you will see, many of the voicings you are about to learn are quite difficult to play with a pick if
you intend to sound the notes simultaneously without tricky muting techniques. For this reason, I
gravitate to using fingerstyle picking for solo guitar.

But, you can still use these voicings with a pick. Some players strike the bass note first with their pick
then follow up with a strum of the upper notes in the voicing – creative techniques like this can open
up possibilities for using a plectrum in a solo guitar setting.

Note that Joe Pass was primarily a plectrum player in his earlier career but moved exclusively to
fingerstyle for solo guitar which became his focus later in his career – presumably because it was
easier to make intricate solo arrangements with the fingerstyle technique.

Check out Appendix 2 of this course for some effective fingerstyle drills if you’re keen to build your
skills in this area.


Bass Lines in Solo Guitar

The key difference between a ‘trio’ chord melody arrangement (to be played in a band) and solo
guitar is the greater prominence of bass notes on the 5th and 6th strings.

You no longer have a bass player to play with, so you need to fill that role ourselves – otherwise, the
arrangement will sound thin and empty.

An example:

Let’s have a look at a passage out of our chord melody arrangement of Fly Me To The Moon that we
completed in the previous module:




   Play audio example 4-1




Notice how thin the arrangement sounds without the rest of the band.


Now let’s add a few solo guitar techniques like bass notes, some arpeggiation here and there, and so
on:




   Play audio example 4-2




As you can see, the arrangements are very similar – I’ve kept most of the voicings the same.

The key difference is the addition of those occasional low bass notes to the voicings on the 5th and
6th strings, and arpeggiation of some of the chords.



 Task: try adding bass notes to the chord voicings you learned previously as I demonstrated here
 – this is an easy way to adapt chord melodies you have arranged to be played with a band to be
 played solo guitar instead.


Key Selection and Open Strings

With the addition of the bass notes, fingerings of chord voicings can be more difficult with solo guitar.
The transition from chord to chord can become burdensome for the fretting hand.

An excellent way around this is to choose a key for your arrangement that allows the use of open
strings – especially for the bass notes.

Choosing a key like A, E, or D gives you essentially another set of free fingers. Without needing to fret
the occasional bass note, you can make things much easier technically.

The other advantage is that open strings enable you to hit bass notes in the lower register when you
play high chord voicings on top.

A common issue with many jazz guitar arrangements is that the bass is too close to the upper
harmony, which results in a thin sound.

For this reason, guitarists like George Van Eps and Lenny Breau added an additional 7th string to the
guitar so that those lower bass notes would be accessible in every position of the guitar.


However, if you are aware of this issue you can still make a very effective arrangement on a 6-string
guitar if you choose your key carefully.

Take for example Ted Greene’s masterful arrangement of Send In The Clowns mentioned earlier in
the course (he tunes the guitar down a half step but otherwise is a standard 6-string guitar):




Alright – that’s the lowdown on solo jazz guitar and how it’s different from arranging chord melodies
in a band setting.

Head to the next lesson now where you’ll discover some very handy voicings for solo guitar with those
juicy bass notes you’ll need for your renditions.


Useful Chord Voicings for Solo Guitar

In solo jazz guitar arranging, you only have 6 strings (and only 4 or so are usually available in a
voicing) – so every note counts.

In this lesson, you’re going to learn about chord voicings that can efficiently convey the sound of the
harmony whilst still being playable.




What is a Shell Voicing?

You may be familiar with common ‘jazz’ bar chord shapes like this:




These kinds of voicings are quite useful for solo jazz guitar. They are familiar shapes with a bass note
on the low strings.

The issue with these voicings though:

Often notes like the 5th and the root are doubled in these shapes.

For pop music, this is an acceptable sound. In jazz, however, so many doubled notes can make the
chord lose its clarity. It also means more unnecessary notes to hold down, creating more work for our
fretting hand.


But here’s the thing:

You can remove the 5th from the chord and still retain the essential character of the sound.

Let’s do this for these shapes, removing the 5th as well as any doubled notes:




All we have left is now the root on the bass, the 3rd, and the 7th in these shapes.

These are called shell voicings – i.e. the ‘bare bones’ of what you need to define the sound of the
chord: the root, 3rd, and 7th.


Shell voicings are one of the keys to playable, great-sounding solo guitar arrangements, as you’ll see
in the lessons that follow.


Useful Shell Voicings for Solo Guitar

Here are the shell voicings that are commonly used in jazz guitar arrangements (note that the 6th
interval can also be used instead of the 7th which gives a warmer, more resolved sound):


Root on the 6th String




Root on the 5th String




Notice how, for some positions of voicings, you can either have the 3rd as the highest note or the 7th
on top instead – it’s good to learn both versions.


SHELL VOICINGS WITH OPEN STRINGS

Always look for opportunities to use open strings for these voicings where possible:

Adding Melody Notes to Shell Voicings

Shell voicings are a convenient way to add a chord underneath melody notes.

Here are some examples of how you could add a melody interval above a shell voicing:


Shell Chord Extensions with Root on the 6th String


·m                        ·m ·111 mm
 X   XX   X   X   X   X   XX   X   X   X   X   X   X XX




          11      'D




          5   13  b7       1 9   b3

      8---8---8---8--�-8---8---8-----�


Shell Chord Extensions with Root on the 5th String


Notes:

– Sometimes the fingering of the shell voicing needs to be adapted (e.g. different fingers used for the
shell voicing or the addition of a barre.)

– Generally, try to avoid doubling notes in the chord – I’ve usually cut out notes from the voicing
when doubling occurs. However, sometimes it still sounds ok so I’ve left doublings in when this is the
case.


Task: study the above chord voicing shapes. Start with the basic shell voicing, then add the
melody notes above as indicated.


Creating Arrangements for Solo Guitar - Moon River

Now we have those handy voicings with bass notes in our arsenal, let’s put them to work on our first
solo guitar arrangement.

Let’s take the first 4 bars of the classic standard Moon River as an example:




   Play audio example 4-3


Check Key and Register

The first thing to look at is the key of the progression and register.

The second bar has an Am7, which is a good sign – I can use an open A on the 5th string as the
bass note in that bar. So I think I’ll leave the key for this arrangement as is.

However, the melody is too low. Let’s shift it up the octave so it’s in a better register to fit voicings
underneath (also remembering to keep the melody notes mostly on the top 2 strings):




   Play audio example 4-4




Perfect – it’s now in a suitable register for the next step.


Add Bass Notes

Before we get ahead of ourselves, there’s an additional step that’s useful for solo jazz guitar
arranging.

To get a feel for where to place shell voicings, a good exercise is to add a bass note underneath the
melody at each chord change, like this:




   Play audio example 4-5




This can give us clues as to what chord shapes might be suitable under the melody at each chord
change.


Analyze the Melody

Just like we did in the previous module, it’s useful to know the interval of the melody notes in relation
to the chord progression:


Apply Chord Voicings

Now it’s a straightforward case of adding chord voicings where I have placed each bass note in the
progression – let’s use those shell voicings we covered in the last lesson:




   Play audio example 4-6




Not bad, but it needs a few tweaks to work well under the fingers. On to the next step.


Refine the Arrangement

Refining an arrangement is the most important step of solo guitar arranging.

Making small tweaks, cutting unessential notes, and looking for skillful ways to finger passages to get
the arrangement to feel good under the hands and sound good is the aim.

This takes practice and involves ‘getting your hands’ dirty on the instrument. Let’s see how we can
refine this passage of Moon River.

Looking at the 2nd bar, the Am7 chord fingering is a difficult stretch with that D in the top:


As mentioned earlier, you can change the A bass note to the open 5th string instead:




Also notice how the shell voicing is doubling the 3rd in the melody when that D falls to a C in the
melody:




This is inefficient and it doesn’t sound very good to have that note doubled.

So let’s resort to an alternative voicing for the D melody note.


Looking at our chord dictionary, you can use this voicing instead:


This works better, but now you have the A root doubled when you place it in the arrangement:




Doubling the root directly above isn’t a big deal and can still sound good, but it is unnecessary.

It also makes it a bit awkward to sound 5 notes at once with my plucking hand using fingerstyle.

So let’s remove the A on the 4th string:




   Play audio example 4-7




Now we’re cooking – that bar sounds and plays much better.


I could keep that voicing for the C on the second beat of bar 2, but after some experimentation I’ve
found this voicing utilizing an open string for the G note:




   Play audio example 4-8




This provides an interesting variety to the sound at this point.

Now looking at the 3rd bar, that F7 voicing is quite awkward there too underneath that B:


You could keep the same position, but instead of a standard shell you could place the 9th (G) above
the 7th instead and cut the A out on the 4th string. This is easier on the hands and has a better
sound:




   Play audio example 4-9


The arrangement sounds a bit empty in bar 3.

Let’s see what happens if you embellish the chord progression and add a V7 chord to the G on the
3rd beat. The last note in the bar (F) has the 7 we need:




  Play audio example 4-10


Now let’s have a look at the last bar.

The final chord seems a bit boring to me as it’s the same chord shape we used at the start of the
arrangement.

So let’s swap out that shape for another one from our chord dictionary:




   Play audio example 4-11


It sounds a bit empty without a bass note though – let’s add a C bass note to that chord voicing:




   Play audio example 4-12




Excellent – that works well now.

As you can see, solo guitar is very much about experimentation and trial and error – starting with
a standard approach and then tweaking through a careful process to listening to the sound and
noticing what it feels like in the hands.

This was a short example but a good case study for you to see what the arranging process is like in
action.

In the following lessons, you’ll discover ways to make a basic arrangement like this sound much more
lush and intricate through a variety of techniques. See you in the next lesson!


Arpeggiation

The next few lessons will give you a playbook of ideas for generating more interest in your
arrangements.

Firstly:

An easy way to get a more impressive sound in your solo guitar arrangements is through
arpeggiation.




  Arpeggiation is when you pluck the notes of a chord one at a time, instead of a block of notes
  played simultaneously.




This can give a more lyrical sound and is especially effective in arrangements of jazz ballads.

Looking again at our 4 bars of Moon River covered in the previous lesson:




   Play audio example 4-13


By adding some simple arpeggiation, notice how much this effects the mood of the arrangement:


Here’s another example of arpeggiation used in the first section of My Funny Valentine:


If you’re keen to build fingerstyle skills so you can arpeggiate chords in this way, see the exercises
in Appendix 2.



 Task: experiment with arpeggiation techniques in your arrangement and notice the effect it has
 on the sound and intent of the music.


Octaves and Diads

Chord voicings are only one way to create interest underneath a melody line.

It helps to have a few more tools in the toolbox to create variety in the harmonic accompaniment.

Octaves and diads are effective devices you can use liberally in your arrangement to create more
interest.

They are particularly useful for faster-moving tunes when you want some padding underneath a
melody without it getting too technically tricky.


Octaves

Wes Montgomery would resort to octaves in his improvised solos as a way to create more textural
interest. We can use this device for our arrangements to great effect.




 Octaves are a case of doubling the melody one octave down.




As an example, let’s look at an excerpt from the classic jazz standard Don’t Get Around Much
Anymore:

When playing the octaves, mute the unwanted open string between the octave notes by gently
touching the string with your first finger.

I tend to strum the strings to give them a powerful sound but other guitarists prefer to individually
pluck each note – experiment!


Diads

Diads are a technique extensively used by Barney Kessel in his solos.




 Diads are when you harmonize a line in a parallel interval, usually 3rds or 6ths.




C Major Scale Diads (Harmonized in 3rds)



   Play audio example 4-14




C Major Scale Diads (Harmonized in 6ths)



   Play audio example 4-15


This is an alternative approach to using octaves and is a softer but richer sound, which sounds very
authentic in the jazz guitar tradition.

Here’s an example of using diads in Don’t Get Around Much Anymore throughout the A section (the
second line has swapped in diads instead of octaves):

Walking Bass Techniques

As mentioned previously, the bass is a crucial element of a great-sounding solo jazz guitar
arrangement.

Earlier in this module, we added some basic bass notes to an arrangement.

Now, let’s look at ways to jazz up those bass lines to create more energy and excitement.

Walking bass is an often overlooked way to create more energy and variety in solo guitar
arrangements.

Joe Pass would often swap out an entire section of the melody and only play walking bass, as a way
to provide some contrast from the constant melody & chords.

Let’s build a walking bassline over a section of Alone Together.




Step 1

Place the root on beat 1 of each bar:




   Play audio example 4-16


Step 2

Now add either a diatonic or chromatic note on beat 4, above or below the following root note:




   Play audio example 4-17




Step 3

To complete your walking bassline, you just need to add two more notes in between the root note of
beat 1, and the approach note of beat 4.

These can be any of the following:

   Diatonic notes from the scale of the given key.
   Chromatic notes, or
   Arpeggios.


Here’s an example:




   Play audio example 4-18




Step 4

Now you can add shell voicings on the root notes on each bar.

It’s good to mix up playing them on the downbeat and on the ‘& of 1’ to create variety:




   Play audio example 4-19


Walking Bass Example: Stompin’ At The Savoy

Here’s an example of this walking bass technique in action over a simple arrangement.

Notice how the walking bass is used to fill out sections where there is a long-held melody note – this
helps keep the drive and swing moving.

It’s particularly effective towards the end of the A Section with that I vi ii V turnaround progression:

In the next lesson, we’re going to look at some more advanced passing chord techniques. I’ll see you
there!


Passing Chords

So far, you’ve learned how to harmonize the melody line at key points in the progression.

However, you’ll come to certain situations where you may want to harmonize additional notes of the
melody or melody notes which are accidentals (notes that are outside of the key).

Let’s investigate a couple of solutions, using the jazz standard There Is No Greater Love as an
example.

Here’s the melody of the first 8 bars with interval analysis of each melody note for reference, and
chord voicings applied at the main points using the process covered earlier in this module:


Play audio example 4-20


Option 1 (Diatonic Notes Only) Use Your Chord Dictionary

If you see extra notes you want to harmonize in the melody, and the notes are diatonic, you already
have what you need from this course.

Simply use voicings from the chord dictionary provided in Appendix 1 to harmonize the melody at
additional points. Although this can make things tricky, the voicings moving through a melody line
creates a very cool special effect:




   Play audio example 4-21


Option 2 (Chromatic Notes) – Chromatic Approach Chords

This is a good choice if you have an approach note in the melody, as is the case in There Is No
Greater Love on the 4th and 6th bars:




In this case, you can use a voicing on the note being approached, then play the same voicing one fret
away for the approach note in the melody:




   Play audio example 4-22


Here’s what the full 8 bars would sound like using a combination of the above passing chord
techniques:


There are more advanced passing chord and harmonization techniques that we’ll cover in the next
module, but the above approaches will give you great results in most arranging situations for now.


CHAPTER TEXT>>>
